coredata.dc:title: Photochemical preparation of fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine nanoparticles for sensitive and selective detection of Hg(II) ions

Sun, Xuping
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we report on a simple, green strategy for the production of fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) nanoparticles (NPs) by UV light irradiation of o-phenylenediamine (oPD) aqueous solution at room temperature for the first time. We further demonstrate the proof of concept that the DAP NPs can be used as a very effective fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of Hg(II) ions with a detection limit as low as 1nM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2,3-Diaminophenazine
Fluorescence
Photochemistry
Sensitive and selective Hg(II) ion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) AFM and (b) TEM images of the products thus obtained.
The MS spectrum of DAP NPs.
1H NMR spectrum of the products dissolved in DMSO-d 6.
UV–vis absorption and fluorescent emission spectra of the DAP NPs dispersion. Inset: the photograph of DAP NPs dispersion under UV light (365nm) ([DAP]=4mM).

(a) Fluorescent spectra of DAP NPs dispersion in the presence of different Hg2+ concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.5, 5, 50, 100, 200 and 500μM; [DAP]=1μM). (b) The dependence of F/F 0 on the concentrations of Hg2+ ions within the range of 0–500μM (incubation time is 20min).

The difference in fluorescence intensity of DAP NPs dispersion between the blank and solutions containing different metal ions (excitation at 437nm; [DAP]=1μM; [M n+]=50μM, F 0 and F are DAP fluorescence intensities at 554nm in the absence and the presence of ions, respectively).

head.ce:title: Photochemical preparation of fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine nanoparticles for sensitive and selective detection of Hg(II) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Lab of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, Jilin, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Lab of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, Jilin, China. Tel.: +86 431 85262065; fax: +86 431 85262065.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: S. Liu and X. Qin contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we report on a simple, green strategy for the production of fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) nanoparticles (NPs) by UV light irradiation of o-phenylenediamine (oPD) aqueous solution at room temperature for the first time. We further demonstrate the proof of concept that the DAP NPs can be used as a very effective fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of Hg(II) ions with a detection limit as low as 1nM.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury(II) (Hg2+) is a well-known poisonous heavy metal, which could easily accumulate in organisms and cause irreversible damage to the central nervous and endocrine system [1–3]. Therefore, highly sensitive and selective detection of trace Hg2+ in the environment is of great importance. Up to now, a variety of techniques, such as atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy, Auger-electron spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, and ion selective electrode or polarography have been used for detection of Hg2+ [4,5]; unfortunately, these methods require sophisticated instrumentation and/or sample preparation, limiting their practical applications. This issue was subsequently circumvented by development of Hg2+ chemosensors based on small molecules [6–9], conjugated polymers [10,11], nanoparticles (NPs) [12–15], biomolecules [16–22], etc. Particularly, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), such as CdS, ZnS and CdTe have been successfully used to construct fluorescent sensors for Hg2+ detection due to their unique optical properties such as broad excitation spectra, tunable narrow emission spectra, negligible photo-bleaching and excellent chemical stability [23–29]. However, these methods suffer from more or less drawbacks such as extra process for surface modification of functional groups, time-consuming, complicated and expensive processes, and serious health and environmental concerns due to the use of heavy metals. Accordingly, developing simple and environmentally friendly methods toward preparation of fluorescent NPs for Hg2+ detection is highly desired.

In this paper, we demonstrate the first preparation of fluorescent 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) NPs by UV light irradiation of o-phenylenediamine (oPD) aqueous solution. We further demonstrate the proof of concept of using DAP NPs as a very effective fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ ions with a detection limit as low as 1nM.

CaCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, Hg(NO3)2, Mg(OAc)2, MnCl2, NiCl2, Pb(NO3)2, and Zn(OAc)2 were purchased from Beijing Chemical Corp. NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, rhodamine 6G and oPD were purchased from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The water used throughout all experiments was purified through a Millipore system.

DAP NPs were prepared by UV light irradiation of oPD aqueous solution at room temperature. In a typical synthesis, 4.33mg of oPD was added into 10mL of H2O, followed by stirring for 30min. After that, the solution was placed under an UV light source (150W) and irradiated for 30min at room temperature. The aqueous dispersion of DAP NPs with the concentration of 4mM was obtained. The detection of Hg2+ ions was performed at room temperature in PBS (0.2M, 7.0) buffer solution. In a typical run, 0.25μL of DAP dispersion (4mM) was added into 1mL of PBS buffer (pH 7.0), followed by the addition of a calculated amount of Hg2+ ions. The fluorescent emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 20min at room temperature.

UV–vis spectrum was obtained on a UV5800 Spectrophotometer. Fluorescent emission spectra were recorded on a RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurement was made on a HITACHI H-8100 electron microscopy (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The sample for TEM characterization was prepared by placing a drop of colloidal solution on carbon-coated copper grid and dried at room temperature. Mass spectrometry (MS) analysis was performed using an Agilent MSD 5975C mass spectrometer. 1H NMR analysis was performed using a VARIAN AS400 spectrometer by dissolving the sample in DMSO-d 6. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was taken by using a SPI3800N microscope (Seiko Instruments, Inc.) operating in the tapping mode with standard silicon nitride tips. The sample for AFM characterization was prepared by dropping the dispersion onto the freshly cleaved mica and air-dried at room temperature.

Quantum yield was measured according to established procedure [30]. The optical densities were measured on UV–vis spectra were obtained on a UV5800 Spectrophotometer. Rhodamine 6G in water (literature quantum yield 95% at 488nm) was chose as a standard. Absolute values are calculated using the standard reference sample that has a fixed and known fluorescence quantum yield value, according to the following equation:
φ x
=
φ
s t d
I x
A x
A
s t d
I
s t d
η x 2
η
s t d
2
where φ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, and A is the optical density, and η is the refractive index. The subscript “std” refers to the reference fluorophore of known quantum yield. In order to minimize re-absorption effects absorbencies in the 10mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength (488nm).

UV light irradiation of oPD aqueous solution leads to a yellow dispersion. Fig. 1 a shows the AFM image of the products thus obtained, indicating the products consist of a large amount of NPs well separated from each other. The height measured from AFM image indicates that the NPs have a size of about 2nm. Fig. 1b shows the typical TEM image of the products, further confirming the formation of NPs about 2nm in diameter. It is worthwhile mentioning that no residues of DAP molecules are observed, indicating the absence of DAP molecules in the solution. Such observation also suggests the formation process of DAP NPs is irreversible and there is no leakage of DAP molecule from the nanoparticle formed.

Fig. 2 shows the MS spectrum of the DAP NPs. The observation of a strong ion peak at 210 indicates that an oligomer in the form of dimer is generated in this synthesis. To determine the chemical structure of the dimer, the corresponding 1H NMR spectrum of the products dissolved in DMSO-d 6 were acquired. Fig. 3 shows the 1H NMR spectrum obtained. It is seen that the products exhibit four peaks with the following chemical shifts centered at (a) δ =7.87ppm, (b) δ =7.53ppm, (c) δ =6.90ppm, and (d) δ =6.25ppm. The multiplets in peak a and b are attributed to the two aromatic protons hydrogens, and the singlet peak c and d are attributed to the two aromatic protons hydrogens and four hydrogens in the two amine (NH2) groups. All these observations indicate the formation of DAP from oPD after UV irradiation [31,32]. According to the previously reported photochemical preparation of poly(p-phenylenediamine) (PpPD) [33] and preparation of DAP microfibers or nanobelts by chemical polymerization of oPD [34–38], the possible formation mechanism is proposed as follows: (1) under UV light irradiation, the electrons are extracted from oPD molecules with the formation of radical cations or semiquinone radicals, which are further dimerized to DAP molecules; (2) the strong π–π stacks, hydrogen bonds, and T-bonded interactions between the DAP molecules lead to formation of DAP NPs, where the T-bonded interactions feature the terminal benzene ring of one molecule and the amine H atoms of its nearest neighbor [34–38]. For the preparation of DAP NPs, two kind of methodologies have been reported previously. The first one is addition of DAP molecules into water [35], and the second one is in situ formation of DAP NPs during the oxidation polymerization of oPD in the presence of oxidant, such as FeCl3 [34]. In this study, we have developed a new method for preparation of DAP NPs by UV light irradiation of oPD aqueous solution. This methodology has some advantages such as green preparation and without the use of chemical oxidants.

Fig. 4 shows the UV–vis absorption and fluorescent emission spectra of the DAP NPs dispersion. The UV–vis spectrum exhibits a strong absorption peak at 417nm, which is similar to the previously reported DAP molecules [38]. It should be noted that a strong fluorescent emission spectrum centered at 554nm was observed when it was excited at 437nm, suggesting the production of fluorescent DAP. The dispersion under UV light (365nm) exhibits an obvious yellow color (inset), revealing that the DAP NPs exhibit strong yellow fluorescence (For interpretation of the references to color in this sentence, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.). The quantum yield is 10.22% using Rhodamine 6G as a reference. In the present study, DAP NPs were obtained by a photochemical method. It is seen that DAP NPs are highly water-soluble and exhibit strong yellow fluorescence, which is similar to the reported DAP molecules [34–36]. Most importantly, compared to DAP molecules, DAP NPs thus obtained are uniform NPs with the size about 2nm confirmed by AFM and TEM images, which can be used as a novel fluorescent NPs for sensing applications.

We first explored the feasibility of using such DAP NPs as a fluorescent sensing platform for Hg2+ detection. Fig. S1 shows the fluorescent emission spectra of DAP NPs dispersion under various conditions. The DAP NPs dispersion in the absence of metal ion exhibits a strong fluorescent peak at 554nm. In contrast, the addition of Hg2+ ions leads to an obvious decrease of fluorescence in intensity, indicating that Hg2+ ions can effectively quench the fluorescence of DAP NPs. It is known that the N-containing groups in poly(o-phenylenediamine) have strong adsorption capacity to Hg2+ ions [39] and Hg2+ can quench the fluorescence of QDs by electron transfer [23]. Thus, the possible sensing mechanism in the present study can be explained as follow: Hg2+ was adsorbed onto the surface of DAP NPs first, and then Hg2+ quenched the fluorescence of DAP NPs by electron transfer from DAP NPs to Hg2+.

For sensitivity study, different concentrations of Hg2+ in the range of 0–500μM were investigated. Fig. 5 a shows the fluorescent spectra of DAP NPs dispersion in the presence of different Hg2+ concentrations. It is seen that the fluorescence intensity of the mixture is sensitive to Hg2+ concentration and decreases with the increase of Hg2+ concentration. Fig. 5b shows dependence of F/F 0 on the concentrations of Hg2+ ions, where F 0 and F are DAP fluorescence intensities at 554nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. The detection limit is estimated to be 1nM (practical measurement indicated in Fig. 4a), which is lower than the tolerance limit of 2ppb (10nM) for Hg2+ in drinking water permitted by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [40]. Note that the detection limit in the present system is much lower than that based on l-cysteine-coated CdSe QD (6nM) [41], N-acetyl-l-cysteine capped ZnS QD (5nM) [23], thioglycolic acid-capped CdTe QD (4nM) [24]. l-Cysteine-coated CdS QD (2nM) [26], and CdTe QD (2.7nM) [27]. Thus, our sensing system exhibits superior sensitivity than previously reported QD systems. The time-dependent fluorescent spectra of DAP–Hg2+ solution (Fig. S2) indicate that only 20min is required to complete the reaction between DAP NPs and Hg2+ ion. It should be mentioned that our sensing system exhibits high sensitivity toward detection of Hg2+, which may be attributed to unique structure and properties of the DAP NPs such as small particle size and abundant N-containing functional groups.

The detection selectivity of DAP NPs toward Hg2+ ions over other representative metal ions, such as Pb2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, and Zn2+ was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 6 . It is seen that a much lower fluorescence was observed for DAP upon addition of Hg2+. In contrast, no tremendous decrease was observed by addition of other ions into the DAP dispersion. We also examined the influence of the concentration of oPD solution on the formation of DAP NPs. Fig. S3 shows the AFM images of DAP NPs obtained by UV irradiation of oPD solutions with concentrations of 2mM and 8mM, suggesting that the resulting NPs have diameters in the range of 2–4nm. The corresponding detection selectivity of various DAP NPs toward Hg2+ ions reveals that the concentration of oPD has negligible effect on selectivity to Hg2+ (Fig. S4). It is found that addition of Ag+ into the DAP dispersion leads to a reduction in the fluorescence by the formation of new compounds between DAP and Ag+ [42], indicating that Ag+ interferes the detection of Hg2+. Fortunately, such issue can be solved by using Br− as a chelating agent for Ag+ [43]. Indeed, the addition of Ag+ into DAP–Hg2+ mixture in the presence of Br− gives no effect on the detection of Hg2+ (Fig. S5). The detection of Hg2+ was also performed in the presence of Ag+ (Fig. S6), revealing that the presence of Ag+ leads to that no good fluorescent peak of DAP was observed. However, the interference of Ag+ can be simply eliminated by addition of Br− into the mixture. All these observations indicate that the present sensing platform can be used for Hg2+ detection with high selectivity.

UV light irradiation of oPD aqueous solution has been proven to be an effective strategy for environmentally friendly preparation of fluorescent DAP NPs for the first time. Such DAP NPs can serve as a fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+. Our present study is important because it provides us novel fluorescent nanomaterials for sensing [23–26], bioimaging and optical imaging [44–46] and other applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21175129), the National Basic Research Program of China (No. 2011CB935800), and the Scientific and Technological Development Plan Project of Jilin Province (Nos. 20100534 and 20110448).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2012.05.089.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

Journal of the American Chemical Society
Journal of the American Chemical Society

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sen Liu received his B.S. in Chemistry (2005) and Ph.D. in Inorganic Chemistry (2010) from Jilin University. After received his Ph.D., he joined in Prof. X. Sun's group as a postdoctoral research associate in State Key Lab of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His current research is focused on the preparation of functional materials and their applications.

Xiaoyun Qin received her B.S. in Chemistry (2010) Tangshan Normal University. Now she is pursuing her M.S. in Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Industry, China West Normal University. Her current research interest is in the area of electrochemistry and electrochemical sensors.

Jingqi Tian received her B.S. in Chemistry (2010) from Jilin University. She is now a graduate student under supervision of Prof. X. Sun. Her current research is focused on electrochemistry, photocatalysis and photoelectrochemistry.

Lei Wang received her B.S. in Chemistry (2005) and Master in Physical Chemistry (2008) from Jilin University. She joined in Prof. X. Sun's group in 2010. Her current research is focused on the nucleic acid detection.

Xuping Sun received his Ph.D. degree in Chemistry from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry under supervision of Prof. Erkang Wang in 2005. His Ph.D. thesis was awarded for National Excellent Hundred Doctoral Dissertation in 2008. He was an Alexander von Humboldt Fellow at Konstanz University, a postdoctoral research associate at Toronto University and Purdue University. He joined in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry as a professor in 2010. He is also an adjunct professor in Chemistry Department and Center of Excellence for Advanced Materials Research at King Abdulaziz University, Saudi Arabia. He has authored or co-authored over 140 papers in peer-reviewed journals. He sits on the Editorial Board of American Journal of Nanotechnology, American Journal of Analytical Chemistry, Biochemistry and Analytical Biochemistry, Journal of Biochips & Tissue Chips, Journal of Nanomedicine & Nanotechnology and the Editorial Advisory Board of International Journal of Genuine Traditional Medicine. His research interests include synthesis and applications of functional materials, bionanotechnology, nanomedicine, electroanalytical chemistry, photocatalysis and photoelectrochemistry.
